note.research: No measurable overall wage effect vs harm concentrated on low-skilled natives / prior immigrants — aggregate vs subgroup, not a true contradiction.
note.simple: One says no overall effect on pay; the other says real harm to the lowest-paid. It's the whole versus a specific group — not a true clash.

note.research: 'Not heavier benefit users' vs a low-skill net drain — driven by the average- vs marginal-cost public-goods assumption.
note.simple: One says immigrants aren't a drain; the other says lower-skilled ones are. The difference is how you count shared government costs.

note.research: Same population: net drain (average-cost) vs net positive (marginal-cost). The accounting assumption alone flips the sign.
note.simple: Same people, opposite results — a cost or a gain — depending only on how you count shared costs.

note.research: Descendants make the long run look positive vs 75-yr projections being assumption-driven and manipulable.
note.simple: Counting immigrants' children makes the long run look good; but these long-range forecasts can be bent to almost any answer.

note.research: US: immigrants ~60% less likely incarcerated vs Europe: positive association for specific offences. Context differs; not a like-for-like clash.
note.simple: In the US immigrants are locked up far less; in Europe some crimes rise with immigration. Different places — not a head-to-head clash.

note.research: European cross-national correlational positive vs German selection-corrected null — a genuine identification dispute about whether the association is causal.
note.simple: One European study finds a link to more crime; a more careful one finds none. They genuinely disagree on whether it's real.

note.research: Economic case (≈no harm overall) and cohesion case (small local trust erosion) point different ways and CANNOT be netted empirically — the weighting is a value choice.
note.simple: The economy looks roughly unharmed, but local trust dips a little. These pull different ways and can't be added up — it's a values call.

note.research: Immigrants produce an outsized patent share vs possible marginal crowd-out of natives in fixed-slot systems.
note.simple: Immigrants invent a lot; but where slots are limited, they might crowd some locals out. Both can be partly true.

note.research: GDP-per-capita / surplus framing vs the claim that the surplus is a transfer from low-wage labour to employers — same facts, distributional reframing.
note.simple: One frames it as the economy gaining; the other as money moving from low-paid workers to employers. Same facts, different emphasis.

note.research: Putnam's constrict claim vs segregation-not-diversity: is the driver ethnic mix itself, or how it is spatially sorted?
note.simple: Is it diversity itself that lowers trust, or the way neighbourhoods are split up? The studies point at different causes.

note.research: The US National Academies evidence (F-wl-1) finds the clearest native wage/employment pressure among prior immigrants and low-skilled natives; the OECD/ILO ten-country synthesis finds the native labour-market impact 'generally negligible' in LMIC destinations. Both can hold — a concentrated distributional effect in the US labour market and a small aggregate effect in labour markets with large informal sectors and different substitutability — but the wage-depression signal does not transport uniformly across destination contexts.
note.simple: US data show the sharpest wage pressure lands on earlier immigrants and low-skilled locals; the ten-country study finds barely any overall effect in poorer countries. The harm is real in some settings and faint in others — it doesn't travel evenly.

note.research: The OECD International Migration Outlook evidence (F-gp-1) supports a positive GDP-per-capita effect in high-income destinations; the OECD/ILO DevEcon synthesis (F-geo-2) reports that immigration is 'unlikely to depress GDP per capita' across ten LMIC destinations. The result appears to replicate across income settings — but the two draw on the same institution (OECD), so this is partially-non-independent corroboration rather than a fully separate confirmation.
note.simple: One OECD study finds immigrants lift income per head in rich countries; a second OECD-linked study finds they don't drag it down in poorer ones. The finding seems to hold across settings — but both come from OECD work, so they don't fully count as two independent confirmations.

note.research: The US-based net-fiscal-drain finding for low-skilled immigrants (F-fl-1) contrasts with the OECD/ILO ten-country result that the fiscal impact is small and mostly net-positive, with a sub-1%-of-GDP deficit in only two partner countries. The divergence reflects different tax-transfer systems, informal-sector shares, and public-goods provision — the 'net drain' conclusion is contingent on the fiscal architecture of the destination, not a universal property of low-skilled immigration.
note.simple: US figures say low-skilled immigrants cost more than they pay in; the ten-country study finds mostly the opposite, with a tiny shortfall in just two countries. It depends on each country's tax-and-benefit system — the 'drain' isn't a fixed fact about low-skilled immigrants everywhere.
